items.q: Does a working prototype prove a design is viable?
items.correct: No - a prototype proves technical function only; viability also depends on cost, market demand and feasibility of manufacture at scale.
items.misconception: Yes - if the prototype works, the design must be viable.
items.random_wrong: Yes - prototypes are only built after viability is fully proven.
items.why: Students equate 'it works' with 'it will succeed'. Many functioning products fail because they cost too much to make or nobody wants to buy them.

items.q: Should evaluation only happen at the end of the design process?
items.correct: No - evaluation is ongoing; designers test and review at every stage so problems are found while they are still cheap to fix.
items.misconception: Yes - evaluation is the final stage, done once the product is finished.
items.random_wrong: No - evaluation should only happen before any designing starts.
items.why: Linear models of designing cause this. In iterative design, each cycle of design-make-test includes evaluation, which steers the next round of changes.

items.q: Is positive feedback from friends and family valid user testing?
items.correct: Not really - valid testing uses representative target users and measurable criteria; friends tend to be biased and may not match the target market.
items.misconception: Yes - any feedback from real people counts as user testing.
items.random_wrong: No - user testing means only the designer tests the product.
items.why: Convenience samples flatter the designer. Useful testing needs the actual target users, realistic tasks and objective measures, not polite encouragement.

items.q: The most objective way to judge whether a design solution is successful is to evaluate it against:
items.why: Correct answer: The design specification. “The designer's personal taste” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which question best tests the COMMERCIAL viability of a design?
items.correct: Can it be made and sold at a price the target market will pay while still covering costs?
items.misconception: Does the designer like the final shape?
items.why: Correct answer: Can it be made and sold at a price the target market will pay while still covering costs?. “Does the designer like the final shape?” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: User testing of a prototype is valuable because it:
items.correct: Reveals real usability problems the designer may not have anticipated
items.misconception: Replaces the need for a specification
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Reveals real usability problems the designer may not have anticipated. “Replaces the need for a specification” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: If testing shows a product fails one point of the specification, the designer should:
items.correct: Modify the design to address the failure, then retest
items.misconception: Ignore the result and continue to manufacture
items.random_wrong: Remove that point from the specification
items.why: It's Modify the design to address the failure, then retest. “Ignore the result and continue to manufacture” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A product costs £4 in materials, £3 in labour and £1 in overheads per unit, and sells for £10. What is the profit per unit?
items.why: The real figure is 2 £. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
